coredata.dc:title: Removal of organic amino derivatives from wastewater by using fluorescent nitrogen-doped GQDs under visible light and fluorescence application under ultra violet light
coredata.dc:creator: Tamizharasan, Selvakumar
Muralidharan, Rajaram
Abirami, Natarajan
Sankeetha, Sasikumar
Arulmozhi, Rajaram
coredata.dc:description: Photocatalysis is one of the most popular organic waste degradation treatments because it can result in the total decomposition of organic pollutants under visible light illumination. Here, we present a metal-free visible-light active catalyst for the effective decomposition of organic aromatic amino derivatives Methylene blue (MB) and Rhodamine B (RB) as model-contaminated dye molecules. Through a one-pot hydrothermal process, blue colour emissive nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were synthesized from urea and malic acid. The physicochemical characteristic techniques were used to analyze the surface morphology, elemental analysis, surface functional groups, absorption, emission, and band gap of prepared N-GQDs. The effective light absorption properties, transfer of electrons, separation of photoinduced charge carriers, and excellent stability with a band gap of 2.69 eV of synthesized N-GQDs have enabled the degradation of Rh B and MB up to 90 % and 86 % respectively, within 120 min of visible light irradiation. Based on extensive active species trapping experiments, a probable photocatalytic dye degradation mechanism is proposed. These N-GQDs can also be used to make fluorescent ink for imaging and security purposes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-GQDs
Rhodamine B
Methylene blue
Wastewater treatment
Fluorescent image applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: SRM Institute of Science and Technology

Department of Chemistry, Aligarh Muslim University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the Department of Chemistry, SRM Institute of Science and Technology , Tamil Nadu-603 203, India.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern of GQDs and N-GQDs.
(a) TEM image of GQDs and (b) TEM image of N-GQDs (HRTEM image of N-GQDs included) (c) Particle size distribution plot for N-GQDs and (d) SAED pattern for N-GQDs.
aUv absorption spectrum of GQDs and N-GQDs. b. The band gap measurement of GQDs and N-GQDs by using a Tauc plot.
a. PL emission spectrum of N-GQDs and b. photostability of synthesized N-GQDs.
FT-IR spectrum of GQDs and N-GQDs.
(a&b) Zeta potential measurements of GQDs and N-GQDs.
XPS spectrum of the as-prepared N-GQD sample: (a) Survey spectrum of N-GQDs; (b) C 1s; (c) N 1s; (d) O 1s.

(a) UV-Absorption spectrum of MB dye under visible light, (b) various concentrations of MB dye, (c) Kinetics study of MB dye (d) UV-Absorption spectrum of Rh B dye under visible light, (e) various concentrations of MB dye, (f) Kinetics study of MB dye.
Possible photocatalytic pathway of N-GQDs and dye molecules.
Scavengers study for Rh B dye.
Fluorescent images without UV light and with UV light.
The atomic percentage of different elements present in the synthesized N-GQDs.

head.ce:title: Removal of organic amino derivatives from wastewater by using fluorescent nitrogen-doped GQDs under visible light and fluorescence application under ultra violet light

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Engineering and Technology, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur 603203, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: SRM Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Engineering and Technology, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, 603203, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Saveetha School of Engineering, Saveetha Institute of Medical and Technical Sciences, Thandalam, Chennai, 602 105, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Saveetha Institute of Medical and Technical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Saveetha School of Engineering, Saveetha Institute of Medical and Technical Sciences, Thandalam, Chennai, 602 105, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalysis is one of the most popular organic waste degradation treatments because it can result in the total decomposition of organic pollutants under visible light illumination. Here, we present a metal-free visible-light active catalyst for the effective decomposition of organic aromatic amino derivatives Methylene blue (MB) and Rhodamine B (RB) as model-contaminated dye molecules. Through a one-pot hydrothermal process, blue colour emissive nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were synthesized from urea and malic acid. The physicochemical characteristic techniques were used to analyze the surface morphology, elemental analysis, surface functional groups, absorption, emission, and band gap of prepared N-GQDs. The effective light absorption properties, transfer of electrons, separation of photoinduced charge carriers, and excellent stability with a band gap of 2.69 eV of synthesized N-GQDs have enabled the degradation of Rh B and MB up to 90 % and 86 % respectively, within 120 min of visible light irradiation. Based on extensive active species trapping experiments, a probable photocatalytic dye degradation mechanism is proposed. These N-GQDs can also be used to make fluorescent ink for imaging and security purposes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Wastewater levels surge due to the excessive utilization of groundwater resulting from rapid urbanisation and industrial activities [1,2]. Based on the wastewater and water sources, can include a wide range of biological, physical, and chemical contaminants, making them potentially hazardous to the atmosphere [3,4]. In recent years, a significant amount of harmful organic chemicals has contaminated pure water. Organic dyes, pharmaceuticals, industrial wastes, and pesticides are only a few examples [5,6]. All of this contributes to pollution in the environment. Harmful by-products penetrate water systems such as lakes, rivers, and seas, and they are decomposed and absorbed in the water causing water pollution [7,8]. Many of these contaminants have been identified as possible human carcinogens and are hazardous to aquatic organisms [9,10]. However, even with increased energy usage, many of these resistant chemical compounds remain difficult to decompose.

These pollutants are produced in the textile, paper, and plastic sectors, among others. On the market, there is a large range of dyes for a variety of applications available in the market [11,12]. Depending on their chemical structures, dyes are classified as Triaryl Methane dyes, Azo dyes, Heterocyclic dyes, Anthraquinone dyes, Reactive dyes, Phthalocyanine dyes, Acidic dyes, Dispersion dyes, Direct dyes, Cationic dyes are all classifications for dyes based on their application method [13,14]. Among the numerous dye groups, anionic dye molecules are much more challenging to remove from water and are normally found as sodium salt forms of sulfonic acids with high water solubility [15]. These kinds of dye molecules have terrible toxicity toward people and other living things. Purification of such dye-contaminated waters is necessary to achieve the required quality of water [16–18].

GQDs (Graphene quantum dots) have attracted a lot of research interest and have exhibited a broad range of potential applications and interesting properties [19,20]. GQDs are mostly single- or multiple-layer products of graphene with a size of under 100 nm (nm) [21,22]. Because of edge effects, quantum confinement, and zero-dimensionality. GQDs show outstanding properties like excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, mild cytotoxicity, great photostability, good water solubility, adjustable bandgap, controlled photoluminescence, and wide surface area [23,24]. GQDs are extremely popular in the areas of material chemistry, physics, biology, and other academic areas, due to the above-mentioned excellent qualities. GQDs naturally have a unique optical characteristic that makes them good building blocks for a variety of photoelectrical conversion, optoelectronic devices, bioimaging, photocatalysis applications, and LEDs (light-emitting diodes) [25–27]. So, it is highly desirable to build high-quality, cost-effective, and efficient GQDs on a broad scale.

GQDs, which are graphene sheets with fewer than 10 sheets and lateral dimensions, have substantial quantum isolation and edge effects [28]. As a result, they have unique physical properties like high spectrum down- and up-conversion photoluminescence (PL), which may be tuned for application areas by manipulating their size, shape, defects, and functionality [29]. For the production of Graphene Quantum Dots, several approaches have been devised, they can be divided into two categories: Methods from the top-down and bottom-up. Large carbon materials, include Carbon Nanotubes, Graphene Sheets, and Carbon fibers, and are sliced into nano-sized Graphene Quantum Dots by physical or chemical techniques in top-down procedures [30–32]. The bottom-up technique is used to synthesized GQDs from organic carbon-based components such as natural products, amino acids, glucose, citric acid, coffee, and tea leaves [33–35]. Their fluorescence emission yields (QY) are improved and their emission wavelengths can be changed by co-doping heteroatoms with GQDs. A few heteroatoms have been introduced into GQDs namely silicon, boron, nitrogen, and sulphur [32,36,37].

Here, we report a simple one-step facile synthesis of acid-derived Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots by hydrothermal treatment. Characterized the prepared compound by using physio and chemical characteristic techniques. The synthesized metal-free N-doped GQDs are used as a photoactive catalyst for the decomposition of aromatic modal pollutants such as methylene blue and rhodamine B. Its degradation up to 86 % for MB and 90 % for RhB measured by UV spectrometer. We also did this same for undoped GQDs for MB dye was degraded up to 44 % in 2 h and this result confirms the dopant plays an important role. Due to the high luminescence and stability of synthesis N-GQDs are used as fluorescent ink for security imaging purposes.

All of the chemicals and reagents used in our experiment were of analytical grade, and we used distilled water (DI) throughout. We also purchased Urea, Malic acid, Ethanol, Rhodamine B (C28H31ClN2O3), methylene blue (C16H18ClN3S), para-benzoquinone (PBQ), tertiary Butyl alcohol (t-BA), polyvinyl chloride (PVC) and sodium salt of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA-2Na) from SRL chemicals and used it without any further purification.

2.68 g (0.02 mol) of Malic acid was dissolved in 40 ml of water with a stirrer for 30 min at room temperature to form a transparent solution. This solution was then transferred to a 100 ml Teflon stainless autoclave. The tightly closed autoclave was kept in a hot air oven and heated to 180 °C for 6 h. To obtain the yellow colour solution, confirmed the formation of GQDs, and the final product was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min. It was then labelled as GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: 2.68 g (0.02 mol) of malic acid and 0.6 g (0.01 mol) urea were diluted in 40 ml distilled water and stirred for 30 min to make a transparent solution after that the solution was then transferred in a 100 ml Teflon-lined stainless autoclave. The closed autoclave was kept in an electrical hot air oven and heated to 180 °C for 6 h. To obtain the brown colour solution, confirmed the formation of Nitrogen-doped GQDs, and the final product was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min. It was then labelled as N-GQDs.

The Rigaku Ultima III instrument was used to conduct the XRD analysis, which obtained data during the range of scanning 2ϴ = 10o up to 80o. CuKα, which has a characteristic wavelength of λ =1.5406 Å, was used to confirm the crystalline or amorphous structure of the produced samples. UV spectroscopy was used to examine the absorption of catalysts at 25 °C using a Shimadzu UV 3600 plus and 200–800 nm is the wavelength range. Utilizing an Energy-Dispersive X-ray analyzer (EDXP). After that, TEM (Transmission Electron Microscopy) was used to evaluate the samples (Model: TEM, 2100 Electron microscopy). With the help of a Kratos Axis instrument and mono-energetic Al K x-rays, the X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) was utilised to examine the chemical composition of N-GQDs.

The organic model pollutant used to evaluate the performance of the synthesized photocatalyst such as Rhodamine B (C28H31ClN2O3) and methylene blue (C16H18ClN3S). A 250 μL prepared photocatalyst was added into 100 mL of rhodamine B (Rh B) 20 ppm solution, and methylene blue (MB 20 ppm). The catalyst was not previously treated before being added to the above-mentioned dye solutions for 90 min in the dark with magnetic stirring to ensure adsorption/desorption equilibrium. The solution was then UV light irradiated by using a 400 W Xenon lamp for 120 min. At every 20-min interval, 3 mL of the dye samples were collected then the collected samples were determined by the intensity absorption of dye solutions by using UV–vis spectroscopy.

The formula for calculating the degradation percentage is.
Dye
degradation
%
=
C 0
−
C t
/
C 0
∗ 100
where Co is denoted as the initial concentration of Rh B and MB solutions at time t = 0 and Ct represents the dye's final concentration at t = t (sec).

For practical applications, the photostability of GQDs is more important. The photostability of N-GQDs was demonstrated by continuously irradiating the solution with UV light (395 nm) for 2 h and collecting samples for PL analysis every 10 min. The fluorescence intensities of N-GQDs showed no noticeable decrease after 2 h of illumination, indicating the compound's excellent stability. The high stability of N-GQDs may aid in a variety of practical applications.

3 ml of synthesized N-GQDs sample was added in 15 mL of 5 % polyvinylchloride (PVC) and mixed well by using the ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. This mixture created a high-thickness ink after one hour of magnetic stirring then the ink was transferred into an empty refill tube, scrawled or drawn on plain Whatman filter paper, and then allowed to air dry. After the paper had dried for an hour, we examined it with UV light at 365 nm and took visible pictures, as shown in Fig. 8.

X-ray diffraction and Raman spectral techniques, respectively, were used to investigate the crystalline nature and phase purity of the GQDs, and N-GQDs as they were initially prepared. Fig. 1 represents the GQDs XRD pattern with a broad peak located at 22.25° characterizes the reflection of (002) planes for graphitic carbon (sp2) in GQDs with an interplanar distance of 0.36 nm [13] and further one peak may be found at 32.8 which related to the (100) hexagonal carbon planes [38] and Fig. 1 shows the XRD pattern of prepared N-GQDs and the broad peak positioned at 25.56o this represents the reflection of (002) planes in N-GQDs with an interplanar distance of 0.35 nm [39]. The amorphous graphitic structure of the synthesized N-GQDs is supported by this evidence. The synthesized N-GQDs' surface functions are likely responsible for the amorphous carbon, which agrees well with previously published reports [40,41].

The surface morphology and particle size analysis of the GQDs and N-GQDs were studied using TEM (Transmission Electron Microscopy) and HR-TEM (High-Resolution TEM). Both GQDs and N- GQDs were closely monodisperse and spherical nanoparticles, as shown by the typical TEM image in Fig. 2a and b. The HR-TEM image of N- GQDs was inserted in the respective TEM images with d spacing values of 0.34 nm. The size distribution pattern agrees well with the Gaussian curve fitting distribution in Fig. 2c. The results of a statistical analysis of more than 100 N-GQDs showed a narrow size distribution in the range of 1–6 nm with an average particle size is 3 nm which is given in Fig. 2c. N- GQDs. Fig. 2d shows the SAED pattern (selected area electron diffraction) of N-GQDs. The very low crystalline character of N-GQDs is evident from the white structure that resembles a circle in the SAED pattern.

Absorption and emission properties of prepared GQDs and N-GQDs were studied using UV and PL techniques. An aqueous homogeneous solution of GQDs and N-GQDs was more stable without precipitation for six months, and under 395 nm UV light irradiation, the GQD's solution shows a blue colour and greenish blue colour for N-GQDs (inset in UV spectrum Fig. 3a), for this property of synthesized compound indicating its potential in the optoelectronic field. The UV–visible spectrum of GQDs and N-GQDs are given in Fig. 3a and GQDs show two peaks at 264 nm and 305 nm which is associated with π-π⁎ transition corresponds to the aromatic rings in GQDs (C=C) and n to π* transition of various functional groups like CO [42]. similarly for N-GQDs two peaks located at 270 nm for π-π⁎ transition (C=C) and a weak broad peak at 307 nm for the transition of n to π* (CO and NH) [39]. Fig. 3b shows the optical band gap was calculated using a Tauc plot which was found to be 3.0 eV for GQDs as well as 2.69 eV for N-GQDs as we revealed by the EDX (Table 1 ) data for N-GQDs, has higher content of O and N in terms of overall atoms, which might introduce more impurity levels in the band gap, and result in the narrow band gap [43,44] to compare with GQDs and this allowing them to be used for photocatalyst for the better performance in the degradation process and also decreased the recombination rate.

Generally carbon-based fluorescent materials, the excitation wavelength dependence emission behaviour is a common occurrence. These actions could be caused by a distribution of different emissive sites on each nanoparticle in addition to effects from particles of various sizes in the sample. The PL emission spectrum N-GQDs is shown in Fig. 4a. The excitation wavelength increases while the emission intensity gradually increases from 280 to 360 nm and then decreases gradually from 380 nm to 480 nm. Maximum intensity is seen in the emission spectra at 360 nm for N-GQDs. Because of the presence of multiple functional groups (FGs) on the surface of N-GQDs emit excitation-dependent emissions. Fig. 4b shows the photostability of N-GQDs under UV light irradiation for 2 h indicating prepared compound has excellent photostability and is useful for real-life applications.

Furthermore, FTIR (Fourier transform infrared) spectroscopy was used to analyze the different types of FGs present in the prepared GQDs and N-GQDs. In Fig. 5 the broad absorption bands observed at 2900–3500 cm−1 in both GQDs and N-GQDs indicated the stretching vibration frequencies of OH and also the transmittance intensity of N-GQDs is a little high compared to GQDs this might be in the presence of NH2 group in the N-GQDs [45]. These results show the presence of some amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of prepared samples, resulting in the GQDs and N-GQDs having a high hydrophilic nature, and the peak observed around 2090–2070 cm−1 corresponds to the CN (imine) [39]. The strong band that appeared in the range of 1700 to 1650 cm−1 will be the corresponding stretching mode of CO and sp2 carbon (C=C-H), indicating that the surface of prepared samples may contain functional groups such as ketone, aldehyde, and aromatic carbon [42,46]. In N-GQDs one strong band was observed at 1540 cm−1 which corresponds to the stretching frequency of NO which conforms to the prepared N-GQDs containing NO2 groups in its surface another peak was observed at 1387 cm−1 which corresponds to the bending mode of = C-H in an aldehyde or NH vibration mode of amide and other broad peak at 680 cm−1 which is indicating N-GQDs contains CC groups on its surface and above spectral data confirms the successful doing of nitrogen in GQDs.

The synthesized GQDs and N-GQDs were dispersed in distilled water and the measured zeta potential of GQDs is −6.01 mV and − 36.0 mV for N-GQDs at neutral PH as given in Fig. 6 . Based on the FT-IR and zeta potential results in this work the GQDs and N-GQDs have negative surfaces in their structure. This property of the prepared sample helps the mono dispersibility of GQDs and N-GQDs for photocatalytic application.

The existence of expected components in the studied structures was also supported by EDX data. Fig. 7 depicts the concentration of each component in the prepared N-GQDs by EDX analysis. Table 1 shows the atomic percentages of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen on the surface of the N-GQDs as they were formed. The EDX measurements demonstrate that nitrogen exists in the N-GQDs, indicating successful nitrogen doping in the GQDs structure.

The presence of various types of functional groups and components of N-GQDs was characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The survey spectrum of N-GQDs shows three strong signals of C1s, N1s, and O1s at ∼286.14 eV, ∼533.37 eV, and ∼ 402.32 eV in Fig. 8a this confirms the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen. The magnified (high resolution) XPS spectra of the C1s (Fig. 8b) show three peaks at ∼284.7 eV for (C=C) sp2, ∼ 286.2 eV for CN (sp3)/ CO (sp3), and ∼ 287.9 eV can be attributed to the existence of the O-C=O/C=N (sp2) respectively. The (HR) high-resolution spectrum of N1s (Fig. 8c) shows three deconvoluted peaks at 399.6 eV, 400.5 eV, and 398.8 eV corresponding to the pyrrolic N, graphitic nitrogen, and pyridinic N respectively. The peaks at ∼531.0 eV for CO [42] and ∼ 532.0 eV for the C-O-C are shown in the magnified XPS spectrum of O1s (Fig. 8d). These results give strong evidence that the synthesized compound of N-GQDs contains Nitrogen and oxygen-containing functional groups on its surface and helps to increase the attraction power of dye molecules and the metal-free green photocatalyst of N-GQDs.

The undoped GQD was employed as a photocatalyst for Rh B and MB model pollutants and did not show much decrease when exposed to visible light. The photocatalytic activity of N-GQDs under visible light was evaluated using the degradation of organic pollutants of Rh B and MB in the aqueous medium. The degradation percentage was analyzed by measuring the intensity changes in UV–vis absorption of Rh B and MB dyes over time. In the absence of a catalyst, the characteristic absorbance indicates that both Rh B and MB resist visible light illumination (Fig. 9a and b). In the absence of visible light, the N-GQDs plays a small decrease in RhB and MB dye's concentration after 120 min with the degradation efficiency of RhB dye ∼20 % and ∼ 10 % for MB dye. Under visible light illumination, the N-GQDs showed a progressive decrease in RhB and MB concentrations over time, with photocatalytic degradation percentages of 90 % and 86 % within 120 min, however, this is indicating nearly completion of degradation. The N-GQDs are inert toward dye solutions in the absence of light, indicating that decomposition rather than adsorption is taking place. Throughout the entire experiment, the strength of the typical Rh B absorption peak, which is located at 550 nm and 664 nm for MB, decreases noticeably (Fig. 9a and d), showing the absence of any additional colorants like chromophore molecules as a by-product. Also, we optimise the concentrations of both dyes (20, 30, 40, 50 ppm) with fixed catalyst concentration results shown in Fig. 9(b&e) indicate when the concentration of dye is increased the degradation percentage decreases. The apparent rate constant of MB degradation with N-GQDs was 0.0335 min−1 and 0.0141 min−1 for Rh B (Fig. 9c and f). Based on their ability to absorb visible light and their abundance of optically active centers, N-GQDs have high photocatalytic properties. N-doping GQD's light absorption across the entire visible-light spectrum is possible to assist in the creation of e−/h+ (electron-hole) pairs and the surface of the N-GQDs could act as electron scavengers and trap the electrons, resulting in inhibiting the recombination of e−/h+ and effective charge transfer for the desired photocatalytic reactions. The maximum capacity of separated holes encourages the formation of very good reactive radicals, which significantly contribute to the organic dye molecules degradation process. The Rh B and MB dyes are cationic, and the zeta potential values indicate that the prepared metal-free Nitrogen-doped GQDs catalysts have negative surfaces. The FT-IR results of N-GQDs demonstrate that surface functional groups such as NH2, -OH, and CONH2 can act as photocatalytic reaction binding sites via effective H-bonding interactions with dye molecules. Furthermore, fast electron transport between N-GQDs and surface-adsorbed dyes could effectively improve electron-hole pair separation.

In the presence of visible light, the metal-free photocatalyst (N-GQDs) in a homogeneous aqueous phase typically produces three primary types of reactive species of superoxide (•O2), hydroxyl radicals (•OH), and holes (h+) which actively contribute to the photodegradation of dyes. To better understand the photocatalytic mechanism and clarify the parts of the main reactive species in the dye degradation process, scavenger studies were conducted. The photodegradation of Rh B was greatly reduced in the current system by the individual administration of PBQ as a •O2 scavenger and tert-butyl alcohol (t-BA) (as a •OH scavenger), which were both thought to work by trapping of respective •O2 and •OH radicals. However, the introduction of Na2EDTA (as an h+ scavenger) led to a minimum decrease in Rh B degradation [50]. The presence of t-BA, p-BQ, and Na2EDTA suppressed the degradation percentages of ∼24 %, ∼36 %, and ∼ 54 %, respectively, showing that •OH and •O2 are the largest contributors to the photocatalytic process, then hole (h+) given in the Fig. 11 . The N-GQDs' structural benefits include a high surface trap density and significant visible light absorption, which make them effective photocatalysts for dye degradation.

Newly synthesized N-GQDs that are subjected to prolonged UV radiation (395 nm) release blue light. Fluorescent ink for imaging and security applications is made with it. As seen in Fig. 12 , anything we write or draw using this fluorescent ink on Waterman filter paper is invisible in visible light and during the daytime. However, when placed under UV light, it emits a blue colour. For security reasons, any important information is written with this invisible ink. Industrial printing techniques can also take advantage of this illuminating ink [45,51,52].

In summary, successfully we synthesized N-GQDs by using a simple and cost-effective single-step facile hydrothermal method. Synthesized compounds were characterized by using Physio and Chemical characterizations. We used easily available malic acid as the source of carbon and urea as a source of dopant (Nitrogen) and prepared N-GQDs to have good optical properties with uniform size and good stability. In comparison to N-free GQDs, N-GQDs exhibit improved photocatalytic performance for removing RhB and MB dyes under visible light irradiation due to their broad visible region adsorption and high separation ratio of photogenerated charges. The degradation percentage of RhB and MB dyes was 90 and 86 respectively for 2 h of visible light irradiation. As a result of the successful doping of the nitrogen element has been improved the photocatalytic activity of GQDs. We have made a reusable, inexpensive, eco-friendly fluorescent ink using these highly luminous N-GQDs that may be utilised for imaging and security applications.

Selvakumar Tamizharasan: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation. Rajaram Muralidharan: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Natarajan Abirami: Validation. Sasikumar Sankeetha: Data curation. Rajaram Arulmozhi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the Department of Chemistry, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Tamil Nadu-603 203, India.
